title: The First Sajjhāy of the Śrī Nandī-sūtra
summary: This is a 24-verse hymn of praise (sajjhāy, a 'sacred-study' recitation) drawn from the opening of the Nandī-sūtra. It first acclaims Mahāvīra — the last of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras and 'source of all scripture' — and then celebrates the four-fold Saṅgha (monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen) through a chain of luminous similes: the Saṅgha as a fortified city, a war-chariot, a wheel, a lotus, the moon, the sun, the ocean, and finally Mount Meru. It closes by saluting all 24 Tīrthaṅkaras and the eleven Gaṇadharas of Mahāvīra. It is recited as study-devotion within the fortnightly Pākṣika pratikramaṇa, kindling reverence for the Tīrthaṅkara and the community that carries his teaching.
ritualPlacement: A svādhyāya (study-recitation), not one of the six Āvaśyakas. It belongs to the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, recited after the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā and before the Dvitīya (second) Nandī-sūtra Sajjhāy, ahead of the Nine Smaraṇas and the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha order). Because the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas have no independent text — they reuse the Pākṣika with the word-substitution 'pakkhī'→'cāumāsī'/'saṁvatsari' and an enlarged kāyotsarga — this sajjhāy is recited in those observances too, by reference. It is a study-portion hymn and does not open the ritual; the Maṅgalācaraṇa and Navkar open the base Devasi pratikramaṇa.
authorAndSource: An extract from the opening maṅgala (auspicious praise) of the Nandī-sūtra, a Śvetāmbara Āgama (counted among the Cūlikā/Mūla group). The received Āgamic redaction is traditionally credited to Devardhigaṇi Kṣamāśramaṇa at the Council of Valabhī (c. 5th–6th c. CE), who committed the orally transmitted canon to writing. The individual gāthās are anonymous within the sūtra; no separate sajjhāy-author is named in the source book.
structureArc: vv.1–2 acclaim Mahāvīra as Jina, world-teacher (jagaguru), kinsman of the world, source of all scripture (suyāṇaṁ pabhavo) and last of the Tīrthaṅkaras. v.3 opens the 'bhaddaṁ' (auspiciousness!) litany to Vīra. vv.4–18 unfold the great chain of Saṅgha similes: v.4 the Saṅgha as a fortified city (nagara); v.5 as a wheel (cakra); v.6 as a war-chariot (ratha); vv.7–8 as a lotus (paüma); v.9 as the moon (candra); v.10 as the sun (sūrya); v.11 as the ocean (samudra); vv.12–18 as Mount Meru/Mandara, elaborated at length (adamantine base, dharma-jewel girdle, golden peaks, Nandana grove, jewel-bright caves). v.19 gathers all eight similes together — city, chariot, wheel, lotus, moon, sun, ocean, Meru — and bows to the Saṅgha, the mine of virtues (guṇāyara). vv.20–21 salute the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras by name from Ṛṣabha to Vardhamāna. vv.22–23 name the eleven Gaṇadharas of Mahāvīra (Indrabhūti through Prabhāsa). v.24 closes with 'jaya' to the teaching (śāsana) of Jina Vīra — the path to liberation that dispels false doctrine.
recurringImagery: The hymn's spine is the four-fold Saṅgha (sādhu, sādhvī, śrāvaka, śrāvikā) imagined through a sequence of sustained similes, each mapping spiritual qualities onto the parts of an object. As a CITY (saṅgha-nagara, v.4): ramparts of the assembly-hall (gaṇa-bhavana), a treasury of scripture-jewels (śruta-ratna), streets of pure right-faith (samyag-darśana), and unbroken walls of right-conduct (akhaṇḍa-cāritra). As a WHEEL (saṅgha-cakra, v.5): hub of restraint and austerity (saṁyama-tapa), irresistible and ever-victorious. As a CHARIOT (saṅgha-ratha, v.6): banner of moral discipline (śīla), swift horses of vow and restraint (tapa-niyama), and the auspicious peal of study-recitation (sajjhāya-nandi-ghoṣa). As a LOTUS (saṅgha-paüma, vv.7–8): risen above the floodwaters of karmic dust (karma-raja), a long stalk of scripture-jewel, firm pericarp of the five great vows (pañca-mahāvrata), filaments of virtue (guṇa-kesara), encircled by śrāvaka-bees, opened by the Jina-sun. As the MOON (saṅgha-candra, v.9): austerity-and-restraint its spotless deer-mark, pure right-faith its cool moonlight, unassailable by the Rāhu of wrong action. As the SUN (saṅgha-sūrya, v.10): rays of fierce austerity that outshine the 'planets' of rival doctrines, radiating the light of knowledge (jñāna). As the OCEAN (saṅgha-samudra, v.11): shores of steadfastness (dhṛti), study-yoga its great sea-creatures, vast and unshakable. As MOUNT MERU/MANDARA (vv.12–18): adamantine base of right-faith (vajra), girdle of dharma-jewels, golden cliffs, the fragrant Nandana grove of good conduct, caves bright with compassion and jewel-herbs — towering, immovable, supreme. v.19 names all eight similes in one breath, so each individual verse may simply invoke its image without re-explaining it.
keyThemes: praise (stuti) of Mahāvīra as last Tīrthaṅkara and source of scripture
the four-fold Saṅgha as object of devotion
sustained simile (rūpaka) — city, chariot, wheel, lotus, moon, sun, ocean, Meru
the five great vows and monastic discipline
triumph of the Jina's teaching over false doctrine

glossary.definition: The four-fold Jain community — monks (sādhu), nuns (sādhvī), laymen (śrāvaka), laywomen (śrāvikā); the central object of praise in this hymn.
Sacred-study recitation performed in the closing study-portion of a pratikramaṇa; not one of the six Āvaśyakas.
A 'ford-maker' — one of the twenty-four supreme teachers who reveal the path; Mahāvīra (Vardhamāna) is the last, Ṛṣabha the first.
The 24th and final Tīrthaṅkara of this era; here hailed as world-teacher (jagaguru) and 'source of all scripture' (suyāṇaṁ pabhavo).
A chief disciple of a Tīrthaṅkara who systematizes his teaching; Mahāvīra had eleven, from Indrabhūti to Prabhāsa (vv.22–23).
'Auspiciousness!' / 'May there be welfare!' — the recurring benedictory refrain that opens the praise of Vīra and of the Saṅgha.
Right faith/insight (Prakrit sammatta) — one of the three jewels; imaged as the city's streets and the moon's moonlight.
Right conduct; the 'unbroken walls' (akhaṇḍa-cāritra) of the Saṅgha-city.
The 'jewel of scripture' — sacred knowledge, imaged as the city's treasure and the lotus's stalk.
The five great vows of a mendicant (non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-possession); the firm pericarp of the Saṅgha-lotus.
Self-restraint/discipline; paired with tapa as the hub of the Saṅgha-wheel and the moon's spotless mark.
Austerity/ascetic practice; its 'fierce rays' make the Saṅgha-sun outshine rival doctrines.
Lotus — the Saṅgha risen pure above the floodwaters of karmic dust (vv.7–8).
The cosmic world-mountain; the Saṅgha as immovable, adamantine-based, golden-peaked (vv.12–18).
The teaching/dispensation of a Jina; v.24 proclaims the triumph of Vīra's śāsana, which destroys false doctrine (kusamaya).

howToRead: A newcomer can simply follow the unfolding similes — read each verse as 'the Saṅgha is like a ___' and notice how every part of the image stands for a spiritual quality, with the eight images gathered in v.19. An advanced practitioner should attend to the precise Prakrit compounds mapping each rūpaka's components onto the three jewels, the five vows, and the discipline of study, and to the canonical roll-call of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras (vv.20–21) and eleven Gaṇadharas (vv.22–23).

sources.title: Nandī-sūtra (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Nandī-sūtra as a Śvetāmbara Āgama and its opening Saṅgha-praise

sources.title: Devardhigani Kshamashramana and the Council of Valabhi
sources.consultedFor: Redaction of the Āgamas at Valabhī and traditional attribution

notes: The hymn is an extract from the opening maṅgala of the Nandī-sūtra; the individual gāthās are anonymous within the canon, and the source book names no separate sajjhāy-author. Verse text, mātrās, anusvāras and conjuncts follow the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book verbatim (e.g. 'ધૂય', 'સિજ્જાય' ~ 'સિજ્ઝાય', 'પડાગુ'); spellings are not normalized. The exact count and ordering of the Saṅgha-similes can vary slightly across recensions of the Nandī opening; the arc above follows the 24-gāthā sequence as printed here. Cited URLs are general references for the Nandī-sūtra and its redaction and should be confirmed against a critical edition where precise wording matters.
